environment_blockers: No Windows OS or Wine/Windows-compatibility runtime is available in this Linux container (`which wine wine64` returns nothing), so builds/Windows/TitanCraft.exe (SHA-256 bbe37ebc86c71dc592f780938f7e03aea47bb6b1cb68acfd36618725914a0cae, 114,594,984 bytes) cannot itself be launched here to prove a native Windows run. This matches the project's own CI split: .github/workflows/ci.yml only performs the native '.exe' launch smoke test on the windows-latest runner job. Remaining manual action: run the exported .exe on a real Windows machine (or via Wine) and confirm it launches, reaches the main menu, and exits cleanly, per README section 27's checklist.

No audio output device is available in this container (ALSA/PulseAudio absent); Godot falls back to the dummy audio driver in every headless/Xvfb run. This does not block any MVP acceptance criterion (audio is explicitly non-priority/deferred per README section 16 and the task's scope freeze) but means audio playback itself was never hearable in this environment.
remaining_non_blocking_work: Raw CI logs (tests/TestResults/*.log, *.trx) are reproducible via tools/test.sh but are not committed, per the repository's own .gitignore (*.log, *.trx); the markdown/JSON evidence files under artifacts/mvp_closure summarize their PASS results instead.

src/SaveSystem/CrashSiteSaveService.cs is dead code (unused by CrashSiteSaveCoordinator or any scene) with its own diverging save-validation rules from LocalSaveGameStore.IsValid; left unmodified per the single-feature/no-unnecessary-refactor rule, flagged for a future human decision (keep, wire in, or delete) — see baseline_report.md.

src/Player/PlayerHealth.cs PrepareRespawn() (full-heal on respawn) is defined and unit-tested but never called by any production code path; the game's actual policy is to restore the exact saved health on reload (already covered by tests). Not a defect, just an unused method worth a human decision on whether respawn should also always heal to max.
OBJ import warnings ('Ambient light ... ignored in PBR') during --import are cosmetic third-party-asset importer warnings, not blocking.
Native Windows launch of the exported .exe still needs to be confirmed by a human on real Windows hardware (or Wine) before a full GO can be declared; this is the only remaining gate.
